title: Data with a Plot
text:
Data with a Plot
      Lindsey E. Wylie, J.D., Ph.D.
      
        Rigorous analytic thinking, made accessible — through unexpected
        datasets, practical methodology, and visualizations that actually stick.

title: Data with a Plot
text:
About

  LEW
  
    My work explores the intersection of law, data, and decision-making —
    with a throughline of measurement: how we decide to count people and
    outcomes, and what happens downstream when those choices get treated as
    facts.

    
      PhD, Social Psychology · certificate in Research Methods & Data Analysis
      JD · concentration in Health Law
      Senior Research Associate · National Center for State Courts
    

    I'm particularly interested in the unintended consequences of legal
    decisions and interventions — the places where a well-meaning rule meets a
    messy world.

    
      ↓ Download CV PDF

title: Data with a Plot
text:
What I Do


  
    Applied Research
    
      Work across law, psychology, and public policy — with a standing interest
      in construct validity, uncertainty, and the politics of how metrics get
      built and used.
    
  

  
    Program Evaluation
    
      Process and outcome evaluations of courts, diversion, and pretrial
      programs — measuring whether an intervention actually does what it
      promises.
    
  

  
    Measurement & Design
    
      Building and validating screening tools and latent-trait measures, and the
      research designs that make a finding trustworthy in the first place.
    
  

  
    Storytelling with Data
    
      Executive and graduate teaching that turns rigorous analysis into
      something a judge, funder, or community will actually read and remember —
      taught on datasets with documented flaws.

title: Writing
text:
Essays
  Writing
  
    On measurement, evidence, and the gap between how decisions
    are supposed to be made and how they actually are.
  






  
    
      Data Storytelling
      8 min read
    
    What Hip-Hop Taught Me About Construct Validity
    When you try to measure something as contested as
    lyricism, every methodological flaw in organizational metrics shows up
    in sharp relief.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Criminal Justice · Measurement
      10 min read
    
    Counted Wrong: Data, Young Adults, and the
    Criminal Justice System
    The categories we use to count people in the
    legal system were not designed with young adults in mind — and that
    design failure has consequences.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Metrics · Policy
      7 min read
    
    When the Metric Becomes the Policy
    How measurement decisions quietly become legal
    and organizational ones — and why that matters for anyone who builds
    or uses KPIs.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Aging & the Law
      9 min read
    
    The Mercy We Wrote Down and Never Used
    Compassionate release exists in almost every
    state's statutes and almost nowhere in practice. What a law that's
    present but unused tells us about the difference between writing a
    policy and measuring whether it works.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Aging · Measurement
      6 min read
    
    How Old Is Old? The Age the Law Pretends to Know
    The legal system fixes hard numbers — 18, 50, 65 —
    onto a boundary that no developmental science draws. A short piece on
    operationalizing a category that doesn't actually have an edge.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Prediction · Risk
      8 min read
    
    Trust the Checklist: Why Expert Judgment Keeps Losing
    Decades of evidence say a two-variable rule
    outpredicts the seasoned professional — in pretrial risk, in clinical
    forecasting, almost everywhere we've checked. Why we keep trusting the
    gut anyway, and what it costs.
    Coming soon
  

  
    
      Data Storytelling · Courts
      7 min read
    
    Cultivating a Court's Data Story
    Most court data dies in a PDF nobody opens. A
    field guide for turning administrative numbers into something a judge,
    a funder, or a community will actually read and remember.
    Coming soon
  




More essays in progress.

title: Teaching
text:
Education
  
  
    Data storytelling and applied research methods for professionals.

title: Teaching
text:
Philosophy
Good data teaching uses datasets with documented flaws, not cleaned-up pedagogical examples. Real data has sampling gaps, contested measurements, uncertain scores, and design choices someone made on a Thursday afternoon without writing them down. That’s what professionals actually encounter — and that’s what I build teaching materials around.
The six concepts I return to most often:

Construct validity Sampling and bias Multidimensional measurement Uncertainty communication Visualization design Narrative framing

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching materials

The Hip-Hop Periodic Table — Data Storytelling Module
A complete 90-minute session built around a dataset of 147 hip-hop artists scored across five lyricism dimensions. Designed for professional and executive audiences. Includes an interactive Quarto dashboard, data dictionary, source bibliography, and facilitator guide.
Concepts covered: All six above.
Deliverables available:

Interactive periodic table — the branded, filterable artist table
Analytics dashboard — companion Quarto dashboard with charts
Data dictionary — variable definitions, rubrics, source bibliography
Excel workbook — full dataset with scoring rubric and era analysis sheets
Facilitator README — session structure, discussion questions by tab







TipSession design note



I open this module by asking executives to rate their favorite rapper on lyricism — 1 to 10, right now, no thinking. After they do, I ask what they just measured. That question takes 45 minutes to properly answer, and by the end the room is arguing about construct validity using their own KPIs.

title: Teaching
text:
Topics available for custom sessions


Measurement and metrics

Building defensible KPIs from first principles
When composite scores help and when they lie
Communicating confidence and uncertainty to non-technical stakeholders
The politics of how organizational metrics get designed


Data visualization

Choosing chart types that match your argument
Designing for uncertainty, not false precision
Interactive vs. static — when each is appropriate
Common visualization mistakes in executive reporting





Get in touch about custom session design, keynotes, or workshop facilitation: lindsey@datawithaplot.org

title: Projects
text:
Work
  Projects
  
    Applied research, teaching tools, and data storytelling experiments.
    Each project is built around a real methodological question.
  




  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    Hip-Hop Periodic Table
    
      A fully documented dataset of 147 hip-hop artists scored across five lyricism
      dimensions, enriched with Billboard chart data, award counts, career metrics,
      and a custom atomic weight formula. Built to teach measurement validity,
      sampling bias, composite scoring, and uncertainty communication — using a
      subject matter executives actually argue about.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Excel / readxl
      Interactive
    
    
    
      Explore the periodic table →
    
  

  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    The Barnum Test
    
      Does your personality type actually measure anything? A 90-second interactive
      experiment that builds you a "personalized" profile, asks how accurate it
      feels — then reveals that everyone gets the identical profile, and that your
      answers were used for nothing. The cleanest possible lesson in construct
      validity, disguised as a party trick.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Construct validity
      Interactive
    
    
    
      Take the Barnum Test →
    
  

  
    Applied Research · Criminal Justice
    Counted Wrong: Emerging Adults & the Crossroads Study
    
      A deep dive into longitudinal data from the Crossroads Study — following
      young people through their first contact with the justice system — to ask a
      question the categories were never built to answer: where does adolescence
      end and adulthood begin? The legal system draws a hard line at 18; the
      developmental data draws nothing of the kind. A project about what happens
      when a measurement boundary gets treated as a biological one.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      Longitudinal data
      Developmental measurement
      Law & policy
    
    
    Coming soon
  

  
    Applied Research · Aging & the Law
    A Right That Exists on Paper: Compassionate Release
    
      Nearly every state and the federal system has a compassionate or medical
      release law on the books — a legal mechanism to release people who are dying,
      gravely ill, or simply too old to pose a risk. Almost none of them get used.
      Drawing on a fifty-state review and the data behind Aging and the Criminal
      Legal System (APA, 2025), this project measures the distance between a
      law's presence and its utilization — and asks what an unused
      statute reveals about how a system measures mercy.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      50-state legal review
      Aging & corrections
      Law & policy
    
    
    Coming soon
  

  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    How Old Is Old?
    
      A 60-second experiment: drag a slider to mark the age where someone becomes
      "old," "a senior," "too old to drive," "too old to start over." Then watch how
      little anyone agrees — and how the law quietly fixes hard numbers (65, 50, 18)
      onto a boundary that doesn't exist in nature. Operationalization made personal.
      It's Counted Wrong at the other end of the lifespan.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Operationalization
      Interactive
    
    
    Coming soon
  

  
    Data Storytelling · Teaching Module
    Trust Your Gut?
    
      Read a handful of real-looking case files and predict who comes back to court
      and who doesn't. Then get scored against a dead-simple statistical rule that
      uses two variables and beats you anyway. The most uncomfortable lesson in
      applied prediction — that expert intuition reliably loses to a checklist —
      delivered as a game you'll want to replay.
    
    
      R / Quarto
      HTML / JS
      Actuarial vs. clinical
      Interactive
    
    
    Coming soon
  




More projects in progress.
